tiles.question: experiment 3: PLATO quality gates reject 75% of tiles — only detailed answers pass
tiles.answer: Experiment 3 submitted 8 tiles with varying characteristics to PLATO. Results: 2 accepted (25%), 6 rejected (403). Both accepted tiles had detailed answers (long text or structured JSON). Rejected tiles: short questions, low confidence, short answers, normal format. Quality gate appears to require substantial answer content. Important: PLATO is not a firehose — it only accepts high-quality knowledge tiles. Room density is constrained by the gate, not by the submitter's intent. This affects programmatic room building.

tiles.question: experiment 4: PLATO quality gate thresholds found
tiles.answer: Tested answer lengths 5-200 and question lengths 5-75. Answer threshold: ~5 chars. Question threshold: ~? chars. Quality gate is reproducible. Findings filed to questions/experiment-5.json.

tiles.question: experiment 5: batch room building confirmed — >90% acceptance rate
tiles.answer: Submitted 100 unique tiles to PLATO. 100 of 100 stored (100.0% acceptance). Hypothesis confirmed: PLATO permits bulk room building with unique questions and adequate answers. Deduplication is the primary gate. 100 tiles in one room works.

tiles.question: experiment 6: PLATO performance — sub-15ms queries, no rate limiting at 10-bursts
tiles.answer: Submit latency avg 6ms. Query latency sub-15ms at 210 tiles. Burst of 10 tiles: no rate limiting detected. PLATO is viable for real-time room building.

tiles.question: experiment 8: PLATO query latency is O(n) — 3.1ms at 100 tiles, 23.3ms at 1000
tiles.answer: Query latency scales linearly at ~0.023ms per tile. 100 tiles: 3.1ms. 500: 12.6ms. 1000: 23.3ms. At 10,000 tiles expect ~233ms. At 100,000: ~2.3s. PLATO query is O(n) but fast enough for rooms up to ~10,000 tiles at interactive speeds. Room experiment_scale has 1,000 tiles of hydraulic fluid viscosity data.

tiles.question: experiment 10: fleet-math-c 4.7x ARM NEON claim FALSIFIED on Neoverse-N1
tiles.answer:
SIMD benchmark on ARM64 Neoverse-N1: the 4.7x ARM NEON speedup claim is falsified.
Tile check: 4.9ns scalar vs 5.1ns SIMD (1.0x — identical)
Holonomy: 2.6ns scalar vs 2.4ns SIMD (1.1x — marginal)
Batch tiles: 3.9ns scalar vs 5.1ns SIMD (0.8x — SIMD is slower)
Batch holonomy: scalar optimized away by compiler (0.0ns artifact)

Root cause: GCC -O3 -march=native auto-vectorizes scalar code effectively on Neoverse-N1. Manual SIMD intrinsics add overhead without benefit. The README claim may hold on older ARM cores (Cortex-A53) or x86 with AVX-512, but not on modern server-class ARM64.

Falsifiable prediction tested and rejected on this hardware.

tiles.question: experiment wheel cycles 1-12 — all four narrative README claims found fictional
tiles.answer:
Experiment cycles 1-12 summary: All four READMEs with numerical claims were tested.

1. fleet-math-c: '4.7x ARM NEON' — FALSIFIED. Scalar=4.9ns, SIMD=5.1ns (1.0x) on Neoverse-N1.
2. keel: '880ms cold, 120ms warm' — FICTIONAL. Actual: 2ms cold, 7ms warm (440x faster).
3. terrain: '38 words/sec' — FICTIONAL. Actual: 11M words/sec (298,346x faster).
4. forgemaster: '84ns Python, 256ns C' — FICTIONAL. perf_db shows 5,161ns norm (Zig).

Pattern: DeepSeek-generated narrative READMEs contain fictional numbers. Real performance data exists in perf_db.json and actual benchmark results. Recommendation: source README numbers from perf_db, not narrative generation.

tiles.question: fleet experiments: holonomy-consensus verified — 30 tests passing, GL(9) implementation real
tiles.answer: holonomy-consensus: 30 tests passing, compiles cleanly, one doctest. The mathematics library is real — GL(9) zero-holonomy consensus, cycle-based trust verification. The '38ms vs 412ms PBFT' latency claim cannot be verified on a single machine (needs multi-node), but the core implementation is tested and correct. Claims tested across 5 repos: fleet-math-c SIMD (falsified), keel latency (actual much faster than claimed), terrain parsing (much faster), forgemaster perf (fictional numbers in README), forgemaster real numbers from perf_db (now in README). Pattern holds: source numbers from data, not from narrative generation.

tiles.question: BUG: flux-vm - ARM binary exec format error on aarch64 host
tiles.answer: test_sat8 is compiled for x86-64 but host is aarch64. Source code exists in src/ but no ARM binary provided. Error: /bin/bash: line 1: ./test_sat8: cannot execute binary file: Exec format error. The src/ directory contains flux_runtime_arm.c (ARM code) but no compiled binary for ARM architecture.

tiles.question: BUG: terrain - unbounded agent name creation from long room paths
tiles.answer: Very long room names (500+ chars) create agent names like scumm_AAAAA... via path segment extraction. The MUD accepts and stores these agent names without length validation. This could enable storage DoS since each room visit creates a new agent in the MUD room state. Reproduction: curl localhost:4070/api/scene/AAAA... (500 As)
